steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a punctuation/boundaries question (Standard English Conventions). The phrase “By supplying oil, grapes, and grain to societies that lacked these valuable items” is an introductory prepositional phrase headed by "By." The decisive rule: an introductory phrase must be separated from the following main clause by a comma only — no stronger mark.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — items; the: A semicolon joins two independent clauses. The introductory phrase is not a clause, so a semicolon here violates the boundary rule and creates an ungrammatical fragment before it. ✗
  • Choice B — items, the: A single comma correctly marks the boundary between an introductory phrase and the main clause that follows. This is the required punctuation. ✓
  • Choice C — items the: No punctuation between the introductory phrase and the main clause omits the required comma; the boundary between phrase and clause is unmarked, violating Standard English conventions. ✗
  • Choice D — items. The: A period ends a sentence. The introductory phrase before “items” is not an independent clause, so a period would create an incomplete sentence (a fragment) before it. ✗
That is the correct answer.
